programs.name: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)
programs.tagline: Comprehensive health coverage including long-term care for Oregon seniors on limited income.

localEntities.name: Local ODHS offices; ADRC centers; CareOregon as coordinated care organization in some regions

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs health coverage or long-term care in Oregon, the Oregon Health Plan provides comprehensive no-cost medical, dental, prescription, and behavioral health services. For seniors needing nursing home care, assisted living, or home-based support, OHP covers these services through its OSIPM program with income limits of $967/month for individuals and $1,450/month for couples.

OHP has multiple tiers with different eligibility rules. Seniors typically qualify through the Aged, Blind, and Disabled category or OSIPM for long-term care needs. The program covers everything from routine medical care to specialized services that help seniors stay in their homes safely. If your parent is over the income limits, spend-down options may still allow qualification.

summary: U.S. citizen or eligible immigration status
Asset limits apply for long-term care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care coverage: must need nursing home care (30+ days), assisted living, adult foster care, or home-based services due to inability to safely live independently
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or eligible immigration status

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at ONE.Oregon.gov or call 1-800-699-9075 — processing time varies but you can apply anytime.

steps.title: Choose your application method
steps.description: Apply online at ONE.Oregon.gov for fastest processing, or call 1-800-699-9075. For seniors with disabilities, call 1-855-673-2372 for specialized help.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income (Social Security statements, pensions), identity documents, residency proof, and asset information including bank statements.

steps.title: Complete the application
steps.description: Provide information about your parent's income, assets, medical needs, and living situation. Be thorough — missing information delays processing.

steps.title: Submit verification documents
steps.description: Upload or mail required proof documents. Keep copies of everything you submit.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies — apply anytime and eligibility will be determined after submission
applicationGuide.tip: Even if your parent's income seems too high, apply anyway — spend-down options and exemptions may still allow qualification for long-term care coverage.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Seniors (Aged, Blind, Disabled Category)
contentSections.footnote: 2025 Federal Benefit Rate limits for ABD category

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Long-Term Care
excluded: Burial plots and prepaid funeral contracts

tiers.name: OHP Plus (ABD)
tiers.coverage: Full medical, dental, prescription, behavioral health

tiers.name: OSIPM (Long-term Care)
tiers.description: For seniors needing nursing home or home-based care
tiers.coverage: All medical plus nursing home, assisted living, adult foster care, home health services

contentSections.text: Seniors often qualify through OSIMP for long-term care even if their income exceeds regular OHP limits. Spend-down options allow qualification by deducting medical expenses from income calculations.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify for OHP long-term care?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home is exempt from asset limits if they intend to return home or if a spouse or dependent child lives there. The home won't count against the $2,000 individual asset limit.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's Social Security is $1,200/month — over the $967 limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through spend-down options. If your parent has monthly medical expenses, those can be deducted from their income for eligibility purposes. Apply anyway — the caseworker will calculate if spend-down makes them eligible.

faqs.question: Does OHP cover assisted living or just nursing homes?
faqs.answer: OHP's OSIPM program covers assisted living, adult foster care, and home-based services — not just nursing homes. This helps seniors get care in less institutional settings while staying in their communities.

faqs.question: If my parent has Medicare, do they still need OHP?
faqs.answer: Yes, OHP can work alongside Medicare as secondary coverage. OHP covers services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care, and eliminates Medicare's copays and deductibles. This is called dual eligibility.

faqs.question: How much money can nursing home residents keep each month?
faqs.answer: Nursing home residents on OHP keep approximately $77/month for personal needs ($90/month if they receive VA pension). All other income goes toward their care costs.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs care now but the application takes time?
faqs.answer: Apply immediately — OHP can provide coverage retroactively up to three months before application if your parent was eligible during that time. Don't wait to apply if care is needed urgently.

programs.name: APD Medicaid Long Term Services and Supports
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication, they may qualify for home care services to avoid nursing home placement.

localEntities.name: Local APD offices and Aging & Disability Resource Connections (ADRCs) statewide via oregon.gov/odhs.

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or managing medications, Oregon's APD Medicaid program can provide home care services to help them stay out of a nursing home. The program covers personal care, homemaker services, home modifications, adult foster care, assisted living, and case management — all personalized based on your parent's specific needs assessment.

This is not automatic coverage. Because funding is limited, there are waitlists, and your parent must meet strict financial limits: $2,982/month income and $2,000 in assets for single applicants in 2026. They must also pass a clinical assessment proving they need Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily help with basic activities like eating, toileting, or medication management) to qualify.

Services are provided through two pathways: the K Plan (state option) or the APD Waiver (for those transitioning from nursing homes or at highest risk). Both require the same financial and functional eligibility, but waiver services have additional waitlists based on priority levels.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment proving your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Must be aged, blind, or disabled
Service Priority Level 1-13 for APD Waiver pathway
Some services require transitioning from nursing home

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Oregon Health Plan or call your local ADRC — processing time varies and includes a required functional assessment interview.

steps.title: Apply for Oregon Health Plan
steps.description: Submit Form 7210 online at benefits.oregon.gov, by phone, or by mail to establish Medicaid eligibility

steps.title: Request long-term services assessment
steps.description: Contact your local Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) or APD office to request functional assessment

steps.title: Complete CAPS assessment
steps.description: Participate in Client Assessment and Planning System interview (in-person or by phone) to determine if you meet Nursing Facility Level of Care

steps.title: Receive priority level assignment
steps.description: If eligible, you'll be assigned Service Priority Level 1-13 based on needs — higher priority gets services first

steps.title: Wait for service authorization
steps.description: Due to limited funding, you may be placed on waitlist until services become available in your area

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; functional assessment via interview (in-person or phone)
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, due to limited funding; varies by eligibility category, service availability, and area. Highest need prioritized via SPL.
applicationGuide.tip: Prepare medical records and a list of daily activities your parent struggles with — the functional assessment is key to qualifying.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits for single applicants. Couples and households have different limits — contact Oregon Health Plan for specific calculations.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if equity under certain limits)

tiers.name: K Plan (State Option)
tiers.description: Basic home and community services for eligible individuals
tiers.coverage: Personal care, homemaker services, case management, basic home modifications

tiers.name: APD Waiver
tiers.description: Enhanced services for those transitioning from nursing homes or at highest risk
tiers.coverage: All K Plan services plus community transition services, housing support, adult foster care, assisted living

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or SSI/SSDI award letters

categories.name: Medical
items: Doctor statements about daily care requirements

contentSections.text: This is not an entitlement program — waitlists are common. Even if your parent qualifies, they may wait months or years for services depending on funding and local availability.

If your parent's income is slightly over the limit, Oregon has spend-down options and spousal impoverishment protections that may still allow qualification — consult with a Medicaid planner before assuming they won't qualify.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is typically exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as the equity stays under certain limits. However, Oregon may place a lien on the home to recover costs after death unless there are protected survivors.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $2,982?
faqs.answer: Oregon offers spend-down options where your dad can qualify by paying medical expenses that bring his effective income below the limit. If he's married, spousal impoverishment rules may also help protect household income.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for services?
faqs.answer: Waitlists vary significantly by region and priority level (1-13). Those with highest medical needs (Priority Level 1-2) typically get services first, while lower priority applicants may wait years in some areas.

faqs.question: What's the difference between K Plan and APD Waiver services?
faqs.answer: Both have the same eligibility requirements, but APD Waiver offers more comprehensive services like community transition help and assisted living options. However, waiver services have longer waitlists because funding is more limited.

faqs.question: Can Mom get these services if she lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, the APD Waiver pathway can help pay for assisted living costs if your mom meets the functional and financial requirements. This is often much less expensive than nursing home care.

faqs.question: What happens during the functional assessment?
faqs.answer: A caseworker will interview your parent (and you) about daily activities like bathing, dressing, eating, and managing medications. They need to document that your parent requires the level of care typically provided in a nursing facility to qualify for home services.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: Complete medical and long-term care under one program with no deductibles or copays if your parent needs nursing facility level care.

localEntities.name: Providence ElderPlace (Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop counties)
Other PACE organizations in Oregon (exact list not in sources; contact ODHS or local AAA)

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing facility level care but wants to stay in the community, PACE provides everything under one program: primary care, specialists, prescription drugs, adult day services, therapy, in-home care, and even nursing home care if needed. Because all care goes through one interdisciplinary team, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate providers — the PACE team manages everything.

PACE is completely free for parents eligible for Medicaid. Parents with Medicare only pay a monthly premium (amount varies by provider). Your parent must be 55+ and assessed at Oregon's nursing facility level of care (Service Priority Level 1-13), which means they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

The biggest requirement: your parent must use only PACE providers for all medical care. No outside doctors or hospitals. PACE is only available in specific service areas — primarily around Portland through Providence ElderPlace.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be assessed at nursing facility level of care (Oregon Service Priority Level 1-13) — meaning your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or similar care
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE-approved service area
Must agree to receive all medical and long-term care exclusively from PACE providers
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot require enhanced care at Oregon State Hospital
Cannot need Medicare skilled care with no community discharge expected within 30 days

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE organization's intake specialists for screening and enrollment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Determine which PACE organization serves your parent's area. Providence ElderPlace covers Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, and Clatsop counties.

steps.title: Call intake specialists
steps.description: Contact the PACE provider directly for initial screening. Each organization handles its own enrollment process.

steps.title: Complete nursing facility assessment
steps.description: Your parent will be assessed to confirm they meet Oregon's nursing facility level of care (Service Priority Level 1-13).

steps.title: Verify service area residency
steps.description: Provide documentation that your parent lives in the PACE organization's approved service area.

steps.title: Determine payment method
steps.description: If Medicaid-eligible, services are free. If Medicare-only, you'll pay a monthly premium. Private pay available for those with neither.

applicationGuide.tip: Before applying, confirm your parent is willing to switch all their medical care to PACE providers only — they cannot keep their current doctors.

categories.name: Care Assessment
items: Documentation for nursing facility level of care assessment (Service Priority Level 1-13)
Current medical records showing need for daily care assistance

categories.name: Residency
items: Proof of residency in PACE service area
Utility bills or lease agreement showing address

offices.name: Providence ElderPlace
offices.address: Serves Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, and Clatsop counties

offices.name: Other PACE Organizations
offices.address: Contact Oregon Department of Human Services or your local Area Agency on Aging for providers in other areas

contentSections.text: PACE is only available in specific service areas and your parent must live there to qualify. If they live outside a PACE service area, they cannot enroll even if they meet all other requirements.

Your parent must agree to use ONLY PACE providers for all medical care — no outside doctors, specialists, or hospitals. This is the biggest adjustment for most families but allows PACE to coordinate all care seamlessly.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctor if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must receive all medical and long-term care exclusively from PACE providers. They cannot see outside doctors, specialists, or use other hospitals once enrolled.

faqs.question: What if my parent has Medicare but not Medicaid — how much will PACE cost?
faqs.answer: Parents with Medicare only pay a monthly premium, but the exact amount varies by PACE provider and isn't specified in available sources. Contact your local PACE organization for current premium rates.

faqs.question: Can my parent live in their own home with PACE services?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as they can live safely in the community with PACE services. Your parent can receive care in their own home, at community-based care facilities, or at PACE health centers.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent needs nursing home care while in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE covers nursing home care if needed, but only at facilities within the PACE network. The interdisciplinary team will coordinate this transition as part of your parent's overall care plan.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to go to adult day services through PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE includes adult day services, but participation depends on your parent's individual care plan developed by the interdisciplinary team. Not all PACE participants use every service.

faqs.question: What if we move to a different county — can my parent stay in PACE?
faqs.answer: Your parent can only receive PACE services in their organization's approved service area. Moving outside this area means they would need to find a different PACE provider in the new location or leave the program.
